caveats: a: No six-digit occupation mix exists for the narrowed lens.
a: Structural ironworkers span industries and structures beyond the selected architectural-metal activities.
a: Observed Claude use is not the same as technical task substitutability.
a: The frozen compensation ratio is LOW quality and uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23819.
rho: RICS respondents were global and only 10% came from the Americas.
rho: The survey measures organizational adoption, not the share of an exposed opportunity successfully implemented.
rho: Integration with BIM, estimating, ERP, and field systems may be harder for smaller firms than for survey respondents.
e: The 71-firm LMM count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE rather than observed firm-level EBITDA data.
e: Public NAICS data do not split receipts or firm counts among the illustrative activities.
e: Narrowing makes eligibility especially sensitive to classification error and multi-trade firms.
s5: FMI surveyed nearly 300 broader engineering and construction leaders, not 238190 firms.
s5: Gallup combines sales and other ownership transfers and reports intentions rather than completions.
s5: Internal transfers can be qualifying control events, but gifts and minority or management changes without control are excluded.
q: Public, commercial, and owner-direct jobs differ in transparency, retainage, and bargaining power.
q: Material and fabrication costs may dominate the package and obscure administrative savings.
q: The sources describe contracting structures rather than an observed five-year pass-through rate.
d5: Neither source isolates the narrowed 238190 service basket.
d5: AIA forecasts nominal spending only through 2027, shorter than the five-year horizon.
d5: Demand differs sharply among data centers, offices, healthcare, hospitality, multifamily, and rehabilitation projects.
o: This measures whether an accountable operator remains necessary, not whether individual steps become automated.
o: Factory preassembly may reduce on-site hours without eliminating the specialty contractor.
o: No five-year technology-substitution study specific to these contractor types was located.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based specialty subcontracting for general contractors and building owners, usually against drawings, specifications, bids, and progress-payment schedules; repair, rehabilitation, and code-driven replacement provide repeat work across a customer and property base.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence to exterior architectural-metal and structural-access installation and repair. NAICS 238190 is a residual category that also includes concrete-form erection and broad on-site welding, whose equipment, customers, work cycles, and economics differ materially from curtain wall, fire escape, stair, balcony, and ornamental-metal contractors.
lens.excluded_activities: Concrete-form erection and dismantling, undifferentiated mobile welding, activities classified to poured concrete, structural-steel/precast, framing, masonry, glazing, roofing, or siding codes, captive crews, shell firms, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors installing and repairing exterior architectural-metal and structural-access systems for external customers, including metal curtain walls, fire escapes, metal balconies and stairways, and ornamental or decorative metalwork, with associated on-site welding.

a.bridge: BLS shows that structural ironworkers read drawings but primarily unload, position, cut, align, bolt, and weld metal, and that 50% of structural ironworkers work for the broader foundation, structure, and building exterior contractor group. Anthropic reports low observed Claude participation for Construction & Extraction occupations. I assign high AI exposure to estimating, bid review, submittals, RFIs, scheduling, purchasing, compliance documentation, and office tasks but little to physical erection, then wage-weight those task shares judgmentally. The occupation and AI evidence are broader than this narrowed industry and likely understate latent use while overstating exposure if LMM firms have very lean offices.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix exists for the narrowed lens.
Structural ironworkers span industries and structures beyond the selected architectural-metal activities.
Observed Claude use is not the same as technical task substitutability.
The frozen compensation ratio is LOW quality and uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23819.
a.change_evidence: Payroll and time-use data from at least 25 LMM curtain-wall, fire-escape, stair, balcony, and ornamental-metal contractors, allocating wages to takeoff, bidding, detailing, submittals, RFIs, scheduling, procurement, project management, fabrication, and field installation, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Core production requires site access, material handling, precise alignment, fastening, welding, and safety control. The exposed opportunity is concentrated in preconstruction and project administration, which are meaningful in specification-heavy architectural-metal work but remain a minority of wages.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 4% growth in ironworker employment from 2024 to 2034 and links demand to future commercial and industrial construction plus rehabilitation of older structures. AIA's July 2026 forecast shows weak, highly uneven nominal nonresidential building spending, including a 0.3% decline in 2026 and 3.0% growth in 2027, with most commercial strength driven by data centers. I convert these broader labor and nominal-spending signals into a near-flat five-year constant-price quantity estimate. Employment may understate productivity gains or overstate quantity when labor supply is tight, while nominal spending may overstate quantity because of inflation.
caveats: Neither source isolates the narrowed 238190 service basket.
AIA forecasts nominal spending only through 2027, shorter than the five-year horizon.
Demand differs sharply among data centers, offices, healthcare, hospitality, multifamily, and rehabilitation projects.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar and physical-unit forecast for metal curtain-wall area, fabricated fire escapes, exterior stairs and balconies, and ornamental-metal installation—split between new build and rehabilitation and reconciled to permits, shipments, backlog, and contract awards—would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: New commercial and institutional projects, rehabilitation, code compliance, and replacement of aging exterior and access systems support activity. High financing costs, delayed projects, volatile tariffs and metal prices, and concentration in a few construction segments keep real quantity uncertain and cyclical.

caveats: The 71-firm LMM count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE rather than observed firm-level EBITDA data.
Public NAICS data do not split receipts or firm counts among the illustrative activities.
Narrowing makes eligibility especially sensitive to classification error and multi-trade firms.
e.change_evidence: A national firm-level classification of all EBITDA-band 238190 businesses using service descriptions, contract mix, payroll occupations, customer concentration, backlog, owner production hours, bonding, and license records would directly estimate the eligible share.
e.rationale: The supplied 71-firm LMM count covers the entire residual NAICS category. A meaningful but uncertain share fits the narrowed architectural-metal and structural-access lens; the rest consists of formwork and broad on-site welding. Within the kept activities, some firms remain ineligible because of owner dependence, single-project or single-GC concentration, weak controls, licensing or bonding dependence, or lack of a transferable operating organization.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23819 ($3,669,150,682) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23819 ($12,506,525,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23819. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $247,003 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238190); class <5: 4,023 firms, avg revenue/firm $397,862 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,670 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,030 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,381,263 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $109,949 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 646 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,982,594 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $237,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 474 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,487,599 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $755,213 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,930,946 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,019,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $62,347,733 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,962,880 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 71

o.bridge: BLS describes ironworkers physically unloading, positioning, cutting, aligning, bolting, and welding metal, often outdoors and at height, and Census defines the selected activities as on-site installation trades. I map this physical and safety-critical task bundle to continued need for an accountable specialty contractor, reserving a small displacement share for factory preassembly, modular systems, GC self-performance, and mechanized layout or handling. Ironworkers include structural work outside the narrowed lens, which may overstate heavy-field content but not the need for site accountability.
caveats: This measures whether an accountable operator remains necessary, not whether individual steps become automated.
Factory preassembly may reduce on-site hours without eliminating the specialty contractor.
No five-year technology-substitution study specific to these contractor types was located.
o.change_evidence: Measured field-labor and contractor displacement from robotic welding or fastening, autonomous material handling, modular façade systems, factory-installed components, and GC self-performance across a representative project sample would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Software can improve design interpretation, logistics, documentation, and inspection support, but a responsible operator must still coordinate lifting and access, verify site conditions, manage trained crews, join and anchor assemblies, comply with code and safety rules, and warrant installed work.

q.bridge: Census describes specialty-trade work as commonly subcontracted from general contractors, and SBA guidance confirms fixed-price construction contracts, specifications, and progress payments. I translate those mechanics into retention of some savings on contracted backlog, then discount heavily for competitive rebids, open-book negotiation, schedule-of-values scrutiny, change-order bargaining, and general-contractor purchasing power. Government contracting rules are only a proxy for broader commercial subcontracting and may overstate formalization.
caveats: Public, commercial, and owner-direct jobs differ in transparency, retainage, and bargaining power.
Material and fabrication costs may dominate the package and obscure administrative savings.
The sources describe contracting structures rather than an observed five-year pass-through rate.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and job-level data linking AI-enabled workflow changes to bid spread, awarded price, gross margin, change orders, rework, schedule penalties, and repeat-customer pricing across multiple rebid cycles would measure retained benefit directly.
q.rationale: An operator usually keeps labor savings achieved after winning a fixed-scope package, but architectural-metal work is repeatedly bid and measured against drawings and quantities. Over five years, customers and competitors can capture gains through lower bids, tighter schedules, and expanded documentation expectations.

rho.bridge: RICS found that only 15% of a global construction-professional sample reported current AI adoption, 31% planned increased investment, and 78% were at no implementation or pilot stage. I use this as a starting point for five-year diffusion into the exposed estimating and project-control tasks, raising implementation above current adoption but discounting for LMM integration, data, skills, safety, and contractual review constraints. The global professional sample likely overrepresents larger, digitally mature firms relative to US LMM specialty subcontractors, so it may overstate near-term implementation.
caveats: RICS respondents were global and only 10% came from the Americas.
The survey measures organizational adoption, not the share of an exposed opportunity successfully implemented.
Integration with BIM, estimating, ERP, and field systems may be harder for smaller firms than for survey respondents.
rho.change_evidence: A multi-year implementation cohort measuring labor hours, bid errors, RFI cycles, rework, schedule variance, and safety outcomes before and after AI deployment in the narrowed contractor types would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Document-intensive workflows can adopt AI within five years, especially bid intake, scope comparison, draft takeoffs, submittal logs, RFI drafting, scheduling alerts, progress-photo organization, and invoice matching. Humans must verify drawings, quantities, weld and fastening details, field conditions, code compliance, safety, and contract commitments.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership in five years, while FMI found that 38% of surveyed engineering and construction owners planned to exit in three to five years. I anchor near Gallup's employer result and use FMI to widen the upside, but discount exit intent because employee/family transactions, gifts, closures, recapitalizations, failed sales, and transfers without an enduring operating business do not all qualify. The surveys measure plans in broader populations rather than completed control transfers in this narrowed lens.
caveats: FMI surveyed nearly 300 broader engineering and construction leaders, not 238190 firms.
Gallup combines sales and other ownership transfers and reports intentions rather than completions.
Internal transfers can be qualifying control events, but gifts and minority or management changes without control are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of deduplicated closed-control transactions for the narrowed activities from GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, union and trade contacts, and specialist brokers, divided by an observed eligible-firm panel, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer scale, specialized crews, backlog, equipment, certifications, bonding history, and GC relationships can support transferability, and construction owner succession pressure is visible. Completion risk remains material because buyers must diligence project liabilities, safety record, labor relationships, owner-held estimating knowledge, working capital, and customer concentration.

principal_driver: Specification-heavy preconstruction and project administration offer focused AI leverage while safety-critical physical installation preserves operator-required demand.
principal_weakness: The residual NAICS is highly heterogeneous, so eligible-firm count, task mix, transaction supply, and demand cannot be observed cleanly for the narrowed business model.

run_meta.title: Other Foundation, Structure, and Building Exterior Contractors

sources.supports: Census defines the residual industry and identifies metal curtain-wall installation, concrete-form erection and dismantling, on-site welding, ornamental and decorative metalwork, and fire-escape installation as illustrative activities; it states that work may include new work, alterations, maintenance, and repairs.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 238190 Other Foundation, Structure, and Building Exterior Contractors

sources.supports: BLS lists physical ironworker tasks and reports that foundation, structure, and building exterior contractors employ 50% of structural ironworkers; it projects ironworker employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to new structures and rehabilitation.
sources.title: Ironworkers

sources.supports: Anthropic reports that physical occupation categories, including Construction & Extraction, are under-represented in both its survey and Claude sessions and that respondents frequently identify judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Cadences

sources.supports: RICS reports more than 2,200 global responses, current AI adoption of 15%, planned investment of 31%, 78% of organizations at no implementation or pilot stage, and skills shortages named as a barrier by 46%.
sources.title: RICS Artificial Intelligence in Construction Report 2025

sources.supports: FMI says its nearly 300-leader engineering and construction survey found 38% of owners planned to exit in three to five years and half of those lacked an ownership transition plan; it also reports employee and family transfer routes.
sources.title: FMI Releases 2024 Study on Ownership Transfer and Management Succession

sources.supports: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; for all working owners, 14% planned to sell, go public, or transfer and 8% planned to close.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: SBA guidance describes construction specifications, fixed-price contracts, lump-sum payment for smaller contracts, progress payments for larger fixed-price contracts and subcontracts, and a 25% self-performance requirement for specialty construction set-asides.
sources.title: Governing rules and responsibilities

sources.supports: AIA reports 2025 nonresidential building spending fell 2% to $846 billion, forecasts a 0.3% decline in 2026 and 3.0% growth in 2027, and shows that commercial strength is concentrated in data centers while other segments remain weak.
sources.title: AIA Consensus Construction Forecast — July 2026

title: Other Foundation, Structure, and Building Exterior Contractors
